results.possibleIdentity: Robert Fiala (also known as Bob Fiala) was an artist and filmmaker active in the late 1960s and 1970s, particularly within the experimental and intermedia art scenes in New York. In the late 1960s, he collaborated on the development of innovative techniques in cinemagraphics and film animation, utilizing Bolex Super 8 cameras, experimental macrophotography, and polarized light. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) community through collaborations with engineers like Steve Bartok and Ted Wolf. His work included projects for Intermedia '68 at the Brooklyn Academy of Music, where he contributed to productions for Carolee Schneemann's kinetic dance group (notably the piece 'Illinois Central'). Fiala was a key member of the Multigravitational Aerodance Group (also known as the Multigravitational Group), an experimental performance collective founded by Stephanie Evanitsky that utilized scaffolding and aerial suspension. In this group, he served as the Company Manager and technical collaborator. During the late 1970s, he was also associated with the Pratt Institute in Brooklyn, where he was recognized in the film department alongside notable figures such as Babette Mangolte and Amy Taubin.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Boardman Howard (1896–1983), often referred to as Bob Howard, was a prominent American sculptor and kinetic artist based in the San Francisco Bay Area. He was part of a famous family of artists and was married to the sculptor Adaline Kent. Howard was active throughout the mid-20th century, and his work remained highly relevant in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is known for his pioneering kinetic sculptures, such as "Semaphore" (1947), and for creating large-scale, industrially fabricated works. In 1969, he was included in the exhibition "California Sculpture" at the School of Visual Arts in New York alongside other major figures of the period. His name appears on lists of artists who worked with the famous fabrication firm Lippincott, Inc. from 1966 onwards—a workshop that was a key partner for artists associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), such as Robert Breer (who designed the kinetic "Floats" for the E.A.T. Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70). Howard also experimented with film, creating abstract works like "Meta" and "Number 6," which utilized flowing colored liquids to explore motion and light. His work is often cited in the context of the San Francisco "6 Gallery" and the intersection of art and technology in Northern California.

Robert Howard (1930–2017) was an American sculptor and professor who served on the faculty of the University of North Carolina (UNC) at Chapel Hill's Art Department. He was active in the 1960s and 1970s, producing sculpture in a studio he shared with other artists in a dairy barn near campus. His work often involved the pursuit of modern forms and materials, and he was a contemporary of the artist and fabricator era that prioritized industrial processes in sculpture creation.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born May 31, 1931) is a Hungarian-born American conceptual artist and a pioneer of environmental and land art who rose to international prominence in the late 1960s and 1970s. Her multidisciplinary practice, which she terms "Visual Philosophy," integrates science, mathematics, philosophy, linguistics, and technology to address ecological and social concerns. Born in Budapest and raised in Sweden, she was educated in the United States at the New School and Columbia University. Denes was a founding member of A.I.R. Gallery in 1972 and is celebrated for seminal works such as "Rice/Tree/Burial" (first realized in 1968), which is considered one of the earliest examples of ecological art, and her iconic 1982 installation "Wheatfield – A Confrontation." During the late 1960s and early 1970s, she was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; she is represented in E.A.T. archival records from 1969 and was featured in exhibitions exploring the intersection of art and information technology, such as "Some More Beginnings" (organized by E.A.T. in 1968) and "Software" (1970).
